---
title: "Model definition: estimate_truncation()"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
bibliography: library.bib
csl: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/citation-style-language/styles/master/apa-numeric-superscript-brackets.csl
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Model definition: estimate_truncation()}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>"
)
```

This model deals with the problem of _nowcasting_, or adjusting for right-truncation in reported count data.
This occurs when the quantity being observed, for example cases, hospitalisations or deaths, is reported with a delay, resulting in an underestimation of recent counts.
The `estimate_truncation()` model infers parameters of the underlying delay distribution from multiple snapshots of past data.
It uses a generative approach where latent untruncated cases are modelled directly and all snapshots contribute to the likelihood.

For a simpler non-Stan approach, see the [baselinenowcast](https://package.epinowcast.org/baselinenowcast/) package.
For more complex settings with time-varying delays, see the [epinowcast](https://package.epinowcast.org) package.

## Model

Given snapshots $C^{i}_{t}$ reflecting reported counts for time $t$ where $i=1\ldots S$ is in order of recency and $S$ is the number of snapshots, we infer the parameters of a discrete truncation distribution $\zeta(\tau | \boldsymbol{\theta})$ with corresponding cumulative mass function $Z(\tau | \boldsymbol{\theta})$.

### Latent case process

Untruncated cases follow a geometric random walk on the log scale:

\begin{align}
\log D_1 &= \alpha \\
\log D_t &= \log D_{t-1} + \sigma_{\mathrm{rw}} \epsilon_t, \quad \epsilon_t \sim \mathrm{Normal}(0, 1)
\end{align}

where $\alpha$ is the intercept and $\sigma_{\mathrm{rw}}$ is the random walk standard deviation.

### Observation model

Expected observations for each snapshot are produced by applying the truncation distribution to the latent cases:

\begin{equation}
\mu^{i}_{t} = Z(T_i - t | \boldsymbol{\theta}) \cdot D_t
\end{equation}

where $T_i$ is the date of the final observation in snapshot $i$ and $Z(\tau)$ is the CDF of the truncation distribution evaluated at delay $\tau$.

All snapshots contribute to the likelihood.
The observation model supports both Poisson and negative binomial families (controlled via `obs_opts()`):

\begin{equation}
C^{i}_{t} \sim \begin{cases}
\mathrm{Poisson}(\mu^{i}_{t}) & \text{if Poisson} \\
\mathrm{NegBinom}(\mu^{i}_{t}, \varphi) & \text{if negative binomial}
\end{cases}
\end{equation}

### Priors

\begin{align}
\alpha &\sim \mathrm{Normal}(\log \bar{C}, 2) \\
\sigma_{\mathrm{rw}} &\sim \mathrm{HalfNormal}(0, 0.1) \\
\boldsymbol{\theta} &\sim \text{as specified by } \texttt{trunc\_opts()} \\
\frac{1}{\varphi^2} &\sim \mathrm{HalfNormal}(0, 1) \quad \text{(negative binomial only)}
\end{align}

where $\bar{C}$ is the mean of the latest snapshot.

### Truncation distribution

The truncation distribution can be any parametric family supported by `dist_spec()`, including log-normal, gamma, and others.
The default is a discretised log-normal.
